era_context: The mid-1880s sat at the peak of the 'New Imperialism,' with European powers racing to partition Africa and extend colonial control across Asia and the Pacific. The Second Industrial Revolution was transforming the economies of Western Europe, North America, and Meiji Japan through steel, chemicals, electricity, and the internal combustion engine. Organised labour was emerging as a political force in industrialised nations, clashing with laissez-faire capitalism. The great powers maintained an uneasy European peace under the Bismarckian alliance system, while settler-colonial states in the Americas, southern Africa, and Australasia were consolidating control over indigenous populations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Sub-Saharan Africa beyond colonial and missionary records
Central Asian communities under Russian imperial rule
Indigenous oral histories across settler-colonial states
Pacific Islands outside major colonial ports
Rural Southeast Asia beyond Dutch, French, and British administrative documentation

events.title: Haymarket affair in Chicago
events.description: On 4 May 1886, a labour rally at Haymarket Square in Chicago — called to protest the police killing of striking workers the previous day — ended in violence when an unknown person threw a dynamite bomb at police, who then opened fire on the crowd. Seven police officers and at least four civilians died; dozens were wounded. Eight anarchist labour organisers were convicted in a sensational trial widely criticised for its lack of due process; four were hanged on 11 November 1887, one committed suicide in his cell, and three were later pardoned by Governor John Peter Altgeld in 1893. The affair became a defining event in international labour history, directly inspiring the designation of 1 May as International Workers' Day, and it severely set back the eight-hour-day movement in the United States for years.
key_figures: Samuel Fielden (defendant, sentenced to death, commuted)
John Peter Altgeld (Illinois Governor, pardoned survivors in 1893)

sources.name: Chicago Tribune and other contemporary Chicago press coverage, May 1886
Trial transcripts, The Haymarket Affair Digital Collection, Chicago Historical Society
Paul Avrich, The Haymarket Tragedy (1984)
James Green, Death in the Haymarket (2006)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in press, court records, and police files. The identity of the bomb-thrower remains unknown and debated.

events.title: Karl Benz patents the Motorwagen
events.description: On 29 January 1886, Karl Benz received German patent DRP 37435 for a 'vehicle powered by a gas engine,' which is widely regarded as the birth certificate of the automobile. The Benz Patent-Motorwagen was a three-wheeled vehicle with a single-cylinder four-stroke engine producing roughly 0.75 horsepower. Independently, Gottlieb Daimler and Wilhelm Maybach were developing their own motorised vehicle in Cannstatt, which they demonstrated later the same year. These parallel developments launched the automobile industry, which would transform transportation, urban planning, energy consumption, and warfare over the following century.
key_figures: Bertha Benz (investor and later famous test driver)

sources.name: German Imperial Patent Office, Patent DRP 37435, 29 January 1886
Contemporary reports in Neue Badische Landeszeitung, June–July 1886
Tom McCarthy, Auto Mania: Cars, Consumers, and the Environment (2007)

events.certainty_note: The patent is extant and dated. Priority disputes with Daimler, Siegfried Marcus, and others are historiographic questions, not doubts about the patent itself.

events.title: Statue of Liberty dedicated in New York Harbor
events.description: On 28 October 1886, President Grover Cleveland presided over the dedication of the Statue of Liberty (officially 'Liberty Enlightening the World') on Bedloe's Island in New York Harbor. The colossal copper statue, designed by Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi with an iron framework engineered by Gustave Eiffel, was a gift from the French Republic to commemorate the Franco-American alliance and republican ideals. The American pedestal was funded by a public campaign championed by Joseph Pulitzer's New York World. The statue became the pre-eminent symbol of immigration and freedom in American culture, though its association with immigration was largely retrospective, cemented by Emma Lazarus's 1883 sonnet 'The New Colossus' which was not prominently displayed until 1903.

sources.name: New York Times and New York World coverage of the dedication ceremony, 29 October 1886
Yasmin Sabina Khan, Enlightening the World: The Creation of the Statue of Liberty (2010)
Edward Berenson, The Statue of Liberty: A Transatlantic Story (2012)

events.certainty_note: One of the most documented public events of the decade. All details firmly established.

events.title: Gold discovered on the Witwatersrand
events.description: In early 1886, Australian prospector George Harrison (or possibly George Walker) discovered gold-bearing conglomerate reef on the Witwatersrand ridge near present-day Johannesburg. President Paul Kruger's South African Republic government proclaimed the Witwatersrand a public goldfield on 20 September 1886. The discovery proved to be the largest gold deposit ever found, and it rapidly drew tens of thousands of fortune-seekers — the 'Uitlanders' — transforming the pastoral Boer republic into the economic engine of southern Africa. The resulting tensions between the Boer government and the predominantly British mining community were a direct cause of the Second Boer War (1899–1902) and ultimately shaped the political geography of modern South Africa.
key_figures: George Harrison (prospector, credited with initial discovery)
Cecil Rhodes (mining magnate, Prime Minister of the Cape Colony)

sources.name: Proclamation of the Witwatersrand goldfield, Government Gazette of the South African Republic, September 1886
Charles van Onselen, New Babylon, New Nineveh: Everyday Life on the Witwatersrand, 1886–1914 (1982)
Jade Davenport, Digging Deep: A History of Mining in South Africa (2013)

events.certainty_note: The government proclamation is dated. The exact date and identity of the first discoverer are debated — Harrison, Walker, and others have competing claims — but the 1886 date is certain.

events.title: Founding of Johannesburg
events.description: Following the Witwatersrand gold discovery, the mining camp that would become Johannesburg was laid out in late 1886. The town grew with extraordinary speed: within a decade it would be the largest city in sub-Saharan Africa. Johannesburg was from the start a racially stratified settlement, with black African migrant labourers providing the cheap labour that made deep-level gold mining economically viable. This racial labour regime, formalised through pass laws and compound systems, became a structural foundation of the apartheid system that would dominate South African politics in the twentieth century.
key_figures: Johann Rissik (Surveyor-General, for whom the city may be named)
Christiaan Johannes Joubert (Chief of Mining, alternative namesake)

sources.name: Keith Gillespie Sobey, The Other Side of the Story: Johannesburg, 1886–1914 (2015)
Contemporary Transvaal government records and surveyor reports, 1886

events.certainty_note: The exact founding date is conventionally given as October 1886, though the settlement grew organically and the precise date is somewhat arbitrary. The etymology of the name is disputed among several 'Johanns.'

events.title: British annexation of Upper Burma completed
events.description: On 1 January 1886, Upper Burma was formally annexed to British India, completing the British conquest of Burma that had begun with the Third Anglo-Burmese War in November 1885. King Thibaw Min and Queen Supayalat had already been exiled to Ratnagiri, India. The annexation eliminated the last independent Burmese kingdom and incorporated the entire country into British India as a province. However, the British faced years of fierce guerrilla resistance from Burmese partisans and ethnic minorities across Upper Burma, and pacification was not completed until approximately 1890. The destruction of the Burmese monarchy and the imposition of direct colonial rule fundamentally disrupted Burmese social, religious, and political structures.
key_figures: Thibaw Min (last King of Burma, deposed and exiled)
General Harry Prendergast (commander of British expedition)

sources.name: Proclamation of annexation, 1 January 1886, Government of India records
Thant Myint-U, The Making of Modern Burma (2001)
D. G. E. Hall, A History of South-East Asia (4th ed., 1981)

events.certainty_note: British administrative records date the annexation precisely. The guerrilla resistance and its suppression are also well documented.

events.title: American Federation of Labor founded
events.description: On 8 December 1886, the American Federation of Labor (AFL) was formally established in Columbus, Ohio, with Samuel Gompers elected as its first president. The AFL emerged from the reorganisation of the Federation of Organized Trades and Labor Unions and adopted a pragmatic 'bread-and-butter' unionism focused on wages, hours, and working conditions rather than radical political transformation. Gompers deliberately limited membership to skilled craft workers, excluding most unskilled labourers, women, and people of colour. The AFL became the dominant labour organisation in the United States for the next half-century, shaping American labour relations around collective bargaining within a capitalist framework rather than the socialist or anarchist models prevalent in European labour movements.
key_figures: Adolph Strasser (cigar makers' union leader, co-founder)

sources.name: Proceedings of the founding convention, Columbus, Ohio, December 1886
Samuel Gompers, Seventy Years of Life and Labor (1925, autobiography)
Julie Greene, Pure and Simple Politics: The American Federation of Labor and Political Activism, 1881–1917 (1998)

events.certainty_note: Founding date and proceedings are fully documented. No scholarly dispute.

events.title: Geronimo surrenders, ending the Apache Wars
events.region: United States / Mexico (Skeleton Canyon, Arizona Territory)
events.description: On 4 September 1886, the Chiricahua Apache leader Geronimo (Goyaałé) surrendered for the final time to General Nelson Miles at Skeleton Canyon in southeastern Arizona Territory. His band of fewer than 40 fighters had eluded approximately 5,000 U.S. troops and 3,000 Mexican soldiers for over a year. The surrender effectively ended the Apache Wars, the last major armed conflict between the United States military and indigenous peoples in the American Southwest. Geronimo and the Chiricahua were sent as prisoners of war to Florida, then Alabama, and finally Fort Sill, Oklahoma, where Geronimo died in 1909 without ever being permitted to return to his homeland.

sources.name: General Nelson Miles, official reports and correspondence, September 1886, National Archives
Geronimo (as told to S. M. Barrett), Geronimo: His Own Story (1906)
Angie Debo, Geronimo: The Man, His Time, His Place (1976)

events.certainty_note: Military records and press coverage provide exact dates. The precise terms of the surrender and whether promises were broken are debated.

events.title: Royal Niger Company chartered
events.description: On 10 July 1886, the British government granted a royal charter to the National African Company, which was reconstituted as the Royal Niger Company under Sir George Taubman Goldie. The charter gave the company administrative authority over the territories along the Niger and Benue rivers, effectively establishing British control over what would become Nigeria through a commercial proxy. The company imposed treaties on local rulers, often through coercion, and monopolised trade along the river system. This chartered-company model of colonialism concentrated power in private commercial hands with minimal metropolitan oversight, and the Royal Niger Company's territory formed the basis of British Nigeria when the charter was revoked in 1900 and the government assumed direct control.
key_figures: George Taubman Goldie (founder and governor of the company)

sources.name: Royal Charter of the Royal Niger Company, 10 July 1886, The London Gazette
John E. Flint, Sir George Goldie and the Making of Nigeria (1960)
Toyin Falola and Matthew Heaton, A History of Nigeria (2008)

events.certainty_note: Charter date is documented in official records. The coercive nature of treaties with local rulers is well established in modern scholarship.

events.title: Henri Moissan isolates fluorine
events.description: On 26 June 1886, French chemist Henri Moissan successfully isolated elemental fluorine by electrolysis of a solution of potassium hydrogen difluoride in anhydrous hydrogen fluoride. Fluorine was the last halogen to be isolated, and its extreme reactivity had defeated or killed several previous experimenters. Moissan's achievement, reported to the Académie des Sciences, earned him the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1906. The isolation of fluorine opened the way for fluorine chemistry, which would eventually produce refrigerants (Freon), non-stick coatings (Teflon), and uranium enrichment processes essential to nuclear technology in the twentieth century.

sources.name: Henri Moissan, 'Action d'un courant électrique sur l'acide fluorhydrique anhydre,' Comptes rendus de l'Académie des Sciences 102 (1886): 1543–1544
Nobel Prize committee citation, 1906

events.certainty_note: Published in the Comptes rendus with an exact date. Confirmed by the Académie's commission. No scholarly dispute.

events.title: Treaty of Bucharest ends the Serbo-Bulgarian War
events.description: The Treaty of Bucharest, signed on 3 March 1886, formally ended the Serbo-Bulgarian War (November 1885 – March 1886). Serbia, which had attacked Bulgaria after the latter's unification with Eastern Rumelia in September 1885, was decisively defeated at the Battle of Slivnitsa but was saved from further Bulgarian advance by Austrian diplomatic intervention. The treaty restored the status quo ante bellum with no territorial changes, but the war demonstrated Bulgaria's unexpected military capacity and its ability to act independently of Russian patronage. Russia's failure to support Bulgaria during the crisis deepened the rift between Sofia and St Petersburg that would shape Balkan alliances for decades.
key_figures: Prince Alexander of Battenberg (ruler of Bulgaria)

sources.name: Treaty of Bucharest, 3 March 1886 (text published in diplomatic correspondence)
R. J. Crampton, Bulgaria, 1878–1918: A History (1983)
Barbara Jelavich, History of the Balkans, vol. 2 (1983)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and diplomatic records are fully preserved. Battle dates and troop movements are well documented.

events.title: Charleston earthquake
events.description: On 31 August 1886, a major earthquake estimated at magnitude 7.0 struck near Charleston, South Carolina, killing approximately 60 people and causing extensive structural damage throughout the city. It was the most destructive earthquake in the recorded history of the southeastern United States and the most damaging seismic event in the eastern U.S. until that time. The earthquake demonstrated that significant seismic hazards existed far from known plate boundaries, challenging contemporary geological assumptions. The destruction exacerbated the already severe economic difficulties of the post-Reconstruction South.
key_figures: Carlisle P. McKinley (U.S. Army officer, wrote early seismic investigation report)
Clarence Dutton (USGS geologist, led federal investigation)

sources.name: Clarence Dutton, The Charleston Earthquake of August 31, 1886, USGS Annual Report (1889)
Contemporary press accounts in the Charleston News and Courier and national newspapers, September 1886
Susan Hough, Earthshaking Science: What We Know (and Don't Know) about Earthquakes (2002)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented. Magnitude estimate is retroactive (instrumental seismology did not exist) but well constrained by damage reports and felt area.

events.title: Coca-Cola first sold in Atlanta
events.description: In May 1886, pharmacist John Stith Pemberton began selling a new carbonated beverage called Coca-Cola at Jacob's Pharmacy in Atlanta, Georgia. The drink was originally marketed as a patent medicine and nerve tonic. Frank Robinson, Pemberton's bookkeeper, named the product and designed the distinctive script logo. The original formula contained extracts of coca leaves and kola nuts. What began as one of many patent medicines of the era would grow into arguably the most recognised consumer brand in world history, and a symbol of American commercial and cultural globalisation in the twentieth century.
key_figures: Frank Robinson (named and marketed the product)
Asa Griggs Candler (later acquirer and builder of the company)

sources.name: Atlanta Journal advertisement, May 1886
Mark Pendergrast, For God, Country, and Coca-Cola (1993, revised 2000)

events.certainty_note: First sale date of 8 May 1886 is attested by contemporary advertising. The full composition of the original formula is debated due to trade secrecy.

events.title: Second session of the Indian National Congress
events.description: The Indian National Congress held its second annual session in Calcutta (now Kolkata) from 27–30 December 1886, under the presidency of Dadabhai Naoroji. Attendance grew significantly from the inaugural 1885 session, with 436 delegates. The Congress passed resolutions calling for reform of the legislative councils, the holding of simultaneous civil service examinations in India and England, and the reduction of military expenditure. While still firmly moderate and loyalist in tone, the Congress was establishing itself as the primary institutional vehicle for Indian political aspirations. Naoroji's presidency was notable for his economic critique of British rule — his 'drain theory' argued that wealth was being systematically extracted from India to Britain.
key_figures: Allan Octavian Hume (British founder of the Congress)

sources.name: Report of the Second Indian National Congress, Calcutta, December 1886
Dadabhai Naoroji, Poverty and Un-British Rule in India (1901)
B. R. Nanda, Gokhale: The Indian Moderates and the British Raj (1977)

events.certainty_note: Congress proceedings are fully documented and published.

events.title: Robert Louis Stevenson publishes Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde
events.description: In January 1886, Robert Louis Stevenson published Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde, a novella that became an immediate bestseller and entered the permanent vocabulary of Western culture. The story of a respectable physician who creates a potion to separate his virtuous and sinful natures resonated powerfully with Victorian anxieties about the duality of human nature, respectability, and repression. It sold approximately 40,000 copies in Britain within six months and was even more successful in the United States. The work influenced subsequent psychology, literary criticism, and popular culture profoundly, and 'Jekyll and Hyde' became a universally understood metaphor.

sources.name: Robert Louis Stevenson, Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde (London: Longmans, Green & Co., 1886)
Contemporary reviews in The Times and The Saturday Review, 1886
William Veeder and Gordon Hirsch (eds.), Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde after One Hundred Years (1988)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and reception are thoroughly documented.

events.title: Abduction of Prince Alexander of Bulgaria
events.description: On 21 August 1886, pro-Russian military officers kidnapped Prince Alexander of Battenberg from his palace in Sofia and forced him to abdicate, then escorted him across the border to Russian-held territory. A counter-coup led by Stefan Stambolov restored Alexander briefly, but Russian Tsar Alexander III made clear his implacable opposition, and Alexander abdicated permanently on 7 September 1886. The crisis demonstrated the depth of Russian determination to control Bulgaria and the limits of great-power patronage over nominally autonomous Balkan states. It led to a prolonged regency under Stambolov and ultimately to the election of Ferdinand of Saxe-Coburg as the new prince in 1887.
key_figures: Prince Alexander of Battenberg (ruler of Bulgaria, deposed)

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence between Sofia, St Petersburg, and Vienna, August–September 1886
R. J. Crampton, Bulgaria, 1878–1918: A History (1983)
Charles Jelavich, Tsarist Russia and Balkan Nationalism (1958)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented in diplomatic archives and contemporary press. The degree of direct Russian orchestration is debated but Russian complicity is undisputed.

events.title: Germany and Britain partition East Africa — Anglo-German Agreement
events.description: On 1 November 1886, Britain and Germany signed an agreement delineating their respective spheres of influence in East Africa. The agreement drew a boundary line from the mouth of the Umba River to Lake Victoria, assigning the territory to the north (broadly, present-day Kenya) to Britain and the territory to the south (broadly, present-day mainland Tanzania) to Germany. The Sultan of Zanzibar's sovereignty was nominally recognised over a ten-mile coastal strip but effectively subordinated to European control. This agreement, reached without the consent of any African ruler or population, was a key step in the European partition of East Africa and directly shaped the colonial borders that became the frontiers of modern nation-states.
key_figures: Count Herbert von Bismarck (German state secretary)
Sultan Barghash bin Said (Sultan of Zanzibar)

sources.name: Anglo-German Agreement of 1 November 1886, published in parliamentary papers
John Iliffe, A Modern History of Tanganyika (1979)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text is preserved. Exact demarcation on the ground took longer and generated further disputes.

events.title: Heinrich Hertz begins experiments on electromagnetic waves
events.description: In 1886, physicist Heinrich Hertz began the systematic experimental programme at the Technische Hochschule in Karlsruhe that would culminate in his 1887–1888 demonstration of electromagnetic waves, confirming James Clerk Maxwell's theoretical predictions. His initial 1886 experiments involved spark-gap apparatus and resonant circuits that he used to detect electromagnetic radiation across a laboratory. While the definitive published results came in 1887–1888, the experimental work began in 1886 and laid the groundwork for all subsequent radio, radar, and wireless communication technology.
key_figures: Hermann von Helmholtz (Hertz's doctoral supervisor, who posed the original research question)

sources.name: Heinrich Hertz, Electric Waves (collected papers, published 1893)
Jed Buchwald, The Creation of Scientific Effects: Heinrich Hertz and Electric Waves (1994)

events.certainty_note: The 1886 start date is established from Hertz's laboratory notebooks and later published papers. The landmark results were published in 1887–1888.

events.title: Riots in Seattle expel Chinese residents
events.description: In February 1886, a white mob in Seattle, Washington Territory, forcibly rounded up the city's Chinese residents and attempted to expel them by putting them on a steamer to San Francisco. Federal troops were called in to restore order, but most of Seattle's approximately 350 Chinese residents were driven from the city. The Seattle riots were part of a broader wave of anti-Chinese violence across the American West following the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882, including similar expulsions in Tacoma (1885) and Rock Springs, Wyoming (1885). These events reflect the virulent racial nativism of the era and the willingness of local authorities to tolerate or participate in ethnic cleansing of Chinese communities.
key_figures: various anti-Chinese agitators including members of the Knights of Labor

sources.name: Seattle Post-Intelligencer and other contemporary newspaper coverage, February 1886
Beth Lew-Williams, The Chinese Must Go: Violence, Exclusion, and the Making of the Alien in America (2018)
Jean Pfaelzer, Driven Out: The Forgotten War Against Chinese Americans (2007)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in contemporary press and legal records. Numbers of expelled residents and exact sequence of events vary by source.

events.title: Meiji government implements modern cabinet system in Japan
events.description: By 1886, the Meiji government under Prime Minister Itō Hirobumi was implementing sweeping administrative modernisation, including the reorganisation of the cabinet (naikaku) system established in December 1885 and the restructuring of government ministries. The year saw the formal establishment of the Imperial University system (with Tokyo Imperial University as its centrepiece), a reformed peerage, and continued preparations for the constitution that would be promulgated in 1889. These institutional reforms were central to the Meiji project of building a modern state capable of renegotiating the unequal treaties imposed by Western powers and achieving parity with Euro-American great powers.

sources.name: Government Gazette of Japan (Kanpō), 1886 — various decrees and ordinances
Andrew Gordon, A Modern History of Japan: From Tokugawa Times to the Present (3rd ed., 2014)
George Akita, Foundations of Constitutional Government in Modern Japan, 1868–1900 (1967)

events.certainty_note: Meiji government records are extensive and well preserved. Administrative dates are precisely documented.

events.title: France completes conquest of Indochina with Protectorate of Annam and Tonkin treaties
events.description: Throughout 1886, France consolidated its control over Annam (central Vietnam) and Tonkin (northern Vietnam) through a series of administrative ordinances and the appointment of Paul Bert as the first Resident-General. The Nguyen dynasty court at Huế was reduced to a ceremonial role. In Tonkin, French forces continued to engage in pacification campaigns against Vietnamese resistance fighters and Chinese irregular forces (the Black Flags). Bert attempted a more conciliatory approach to Vietnamese elites but died of dysentery in November 1886, and the colonial administration subsequently hardened. France's consolidation of Indochina was part of its broader imperial competition with Britain in Southeast Asia.
key_figures: Paul Bert (Resident-General, died in office November 1886)
Emperor Đồng Khánh (Nguyen dynasty, French-installed puppet emperor)
Tôn Thất Thuyết (leader of anti-French resistance)

sources.name: Paul Bert, official correspondence and reports as Resident-General, 1886
Pierre Brocheux and Daniel Hémery, Indochina: An Ambiguous Colonization, 1858–1954 (2009, English trans.)

events.certainty_note: French colonial administrative records are extensive. Vietnamese resistance is well documented in both French and Vietnamese sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly repeated claims about 1886 warrant qualification. (1) Karl Benz is often credited as the sole 'inventor of the automobile,' but this oversimplifies a complex history: Gottlieb Daimler and Wilhelm Maybach independently built a motorised vehicle in the same year, and earlier claimants include Siegfried Marcus (Vienna, 1870s) and various steam-powered vehicle builders. The Benz patent is best understood as a key legal milestone rather than an unambiguous 'first.' (2) The Haymarket affair is sometimes presented as straightforwardly proving a 'police conspiracy' or, conversely, a genuine 'anarchist bomb plot.' Modern scholarship (Avrich, Green) emphasises that the bomber's identity remains unknown and that the trial was deeply flawed regardless of who threw the bomb — the convictions were based on the defendants' political beliefs rather than evidence of direct involvement. (3) The Statue of Liberty's association with immigration is often treated as original to its conception, but the statue was designed as a monument to Franco-American republican friendship, not immigration. Its reinterpretation as a symbol of welcome to immigrants was a later cultural process, significantly advanced only when Lazarus's poem was mounted on the pedestal in 1903. (4) The founding of Johannesburg is sometimes given a precise date, but the settlement grew organically from mining camps and no single founding moment can be identified with precision; the conventional date is somewhat arbitrary.
historiographic_note: The year 1886 is richly documented across most regions covered, with extensive newspaper archives, government records, diplomatic correspondence, and photographic evidence available for Europe, North America, Japan, and colonial administrations globally. The main biases in the surviving record are those typical of the late nineteenth century: sources overwhelmingly reflect the perspectives of colonial administrators, European diplomats, and literate elites. African, Southeast Asian, and indigenous American perspectives are largely available only through the filter of colonial documentation, missionary reports, or much later oral history projects. The experience of ordinary workers, women, and colonised peoples is significantly underrepresented relative to that of political leaders and industrialists.

graph_edges.note: The Haymarket affair and its martyrs directly inspired the 1889 Second International's designation of May Day as International Workers' Day
The Benz patent launched the automobile era that Ford's mass production would transform
Witwatersrand gold and the resulting Uitlander tensions were a primary cause of the Second Boer War
The annexation directly followed the military conquest of November 1885
The AFL's founding was shaped by the same labour upheaval that produced the Haymarket affair
The Royal Niger Company charter implemented the spheres of influence negotiated at Berlin
The Serbo-Bulgarian War was triggered by Bulgarian unification with Eastern Rumelia
The second session continued the institutional development begun at the 1885 founding
Both events were part of the Bulgarian crisis of 1885–1887 involving Russian interference in Balkan affairs
The Anglo-German partition of East Africa implemented principles established at the Berlin Conference
Hertz's 1886 experimental programme produced the landmark electromagnetic wave demonstrations of 1887–1888
Anti-Chinese violence was enabled and encouraged by the legal framework of exclusion
Administrative reforms of 1886 were preparatory steps toward the 1889 constitution
